quick_facts.label: Typical teaching stage
quick_facts.value: This role commonly spans upper-primary/middle school (Classes VI–VIII) and secondary/high school (Classes IX–X); exact class allocation and subjects are set by the school.

quick_facts.label: Core qualification route
quick_facts.value: For the secondary/high-school level, NCTE’s qualification schedule lists a graduate degree plus B.Ed. (or equivalent), with a four-year integrated B.Sc.-B.Ed. route also listed.

quick_facts.label: Classes VI–VIII eligibility
quick_facts.value: For appointments covered by the RTE framework for Classes I–VIII, passing the Teacher Eligibility Test (TET) is an essential qualification; the appropriate recruiting authority ultimately verifies eligibility.

quick_facts.label: Qualification rules vary by school and state
quick_facts.value: For CBSE-affiliated schools, teaching-staff qualifications must follow NCTE regulations as amended and the applicable government recruitment rules; those rules can prevail where relevant.

quick_facts.label: Professional preparation
quick_facts.value: NCTE describes B.Ed. as preparation for upper-primary, secondary and senior-secondary teaching; its 2014 reforms made the full-time B.Ed. programme two years and include theory, practicum and school internship.

sections.heading: Responsibilities
paragraphs: A secondary teacher plans and completes curriculum work, monitors students’ learning, gives additional instruction where needed, and communicates progress to parents or guardians. These duties are expressly set out for teachers appointed under the Right to Education Act’s framework and are especially relevant to the role’s Classes VI–VIII component.

In a CBSE-affiliated school, classroom work sits within the school’s curriculum, pedagogy and evaluation requirements. CBSE’s affiliation materials also require timely scrutiny, assessment and correction of pupils’ written work and home assignments; school records include assessment materials and attendance records for Classes IX and X where applicable.
bullets: Teach an allocated school subject or subjects using the school’s prescribed curriculum and timetable.
Assess learning, give feedback and provide supplementary support when learners need it.
Maintain regular attendance and punctuality, communicate with families, and contribute to required academic documentation.
Support experiential, digital, life-skills and inclusive school activities where these form part of the school programme.

sections.heading: Entry routes and eligibility checks
paragraphs: A standard route to teaching Classes IX–X is a relevant graduate degree followed by B.Ed., or an integrated B.Sc.-B.Ed. route. Because this node also includes Classes VI–VIII, applicants should distinguish the grade band in the vacancy: the CTET authority states that the NCTE’s RTE-related minimum qualifications for Classes I–VIII include passing the TET conducted by the appropriate government.

A B.Ed. is an important professional credential but is not, on its own, a universal hiring guarantee. CBSE states that qualifications in affiliated schools are governed by NCTE regulations as amended, applicable government recruitment rules, and subject-specific requirements. Candidates should therefore check the vacancy notice, board affiliation, state or UT rules, required teaching subject, language requirements, and whether CTET or the relevant State TET is requested before applying.

Choose an NCTE-recognised teacher-education programme and retain degree, marksheet, internship and TET documentation. NCTE’s published B.Ed. framework describes preparation across Classes VI–XII and includes school-based practical preparation.
bullets: Earn the academic degree appropriate to the subject and grade band sought.
Complete B.Ed. or an accepted integrated teacher-education qualification.
For Classes VI–VIII posts within the RTE framework, obtain TET eligibility as required by the appropriate government.
Match the individual school’s advertised subject, board, experience, language and document requirements.

sections.heading: Skills developed and used in the role
paragraphs: The role requires subject knowledge combined with pedagogy: planning instruction, managing learning activities, assessing student work and adapting instruction after observing each learner’s progress. NCTE identifies theory, practicum and internship as core components of teacher preparation, while the statutory teacher duties emphasise assessment and additional instruction.

Digital and inclusive teaching capability is increasingly relevant in CBSE-affiliated settings. CBSE’s affiliation requirements identify digital education, experiential learning, life-skills education and value education as school curriculum responsibilities; NCTE’s teacher-education reforms identify ICT, gender and disability/inclusive education as integral programme elements.
bullets: Subject explanation and lesson design appropriate to adolescent learners.
Formative assessment, feedback, written-work review and record keeping.
Classroom communication and constructive communication with parents or guardians.
Inclusive, gender-sensitive and digitally supported teaching practices.
Ability to use experiential activities and adjust support to learner needs.

sections.heading: Workplace and employment context
paragraphs: A private secondary teacher is employed by a privately managed school rather than through a government-school recruitment cadre. Work is school-based and combines classroom teaching with assessment, student records, family communication, meetings and school activities. The exact calendar, workload, class sizes, subjects and benefits depend on the institution and the state or UT.

CBSE-affiliated private schools provide one important employment setting, but they are not the whole private-school market. For such schools, CBSE requires trained and qualified teaching staff on a regular basis and lists infrastructure, safety, staff records and curriculum-related obligations that shape the teaching environment.
bullets: Potential settings include independent private schools and private schools affiliated with boards such as CBSE; vacancy requirements differ by institution.
Teaching generally combines instructional time with preparation, assessment, parent interaction and school documentation.
In CBSE-affiliated schools, teachers may also be asked to support Board-linked academic or evaluation work when applicable.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: There is no single India-wide salary figure for this private-sector role: pay, allowances, workload and benefits vary by school management, location, board affiliation, subject demand, experience and the applicable state or UT framework. Candidates should treat any advertised salary as vacancy-specific and review the appointment letter and applicable local rules.

For CBSE-affiliated schools, the Board identifies payment of salary and other allowances in accordance with state-government norms as a mandatory requirement. This is an institutional compliance expectation, not a nationwide published pay scale for every private secondary teacher.
bullets: Compare salary alongside provident-fund coverage, insurance, leave, teaching load, transport or housing benefits, and notice-period terms where offered.
Verify whether the school’s stated pay and allowances align with the applicable state or UT norms and the written contract.
Use dated vacancy advertisements only as local market context, not as a national salary benchmark.

India Code: Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
